title: September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 | Stats NZ
content:
## September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
03 December 2021, 10:45am
September 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19 provides technical users with information about our approach to measuring the impact of COVID-19 in the forthcoming release of Gross domestic product: September 2021 quarter (scheduled for 16 December 2021).
10 December 2021 : We have added Change to COVID-19 alternative indicator used for heritage and artistic activities and Changes to components using alternative data sources due to COVID-19 to this paper.
This paper provides information about how we are responding to ongoing measurement challenges faced due to the nature of the economic shock caused by COVID-19. This paper follows similar updates about our methodology during COVID-19 that have been published in advance of the release of gross domestic product.
Information is presented in the following sections:
- Key events in the September 2021 quarter
- Data and methodology considerations
- Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
- Corrections to quarterly gross domestic product
Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments is an updated guide to the concepts and methods we are using to compile gross domestic product (GDP) during COVID-19 in New Zealand.

### Key events in the September 2021 quarter
Globally, the COVID-19 pandemic continued to affect travel flows and economic activity. New Zealand’s border remained closed to most travelers throughout the quarter, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel. Quarantine-free travel between Australia and New Zealand was in place from 19 April 2021 to 23 July 2021. There were only 23 days of quarantine-free travel in the September 2021 quarter compared with 73 days in the June 2021 quarter. Economic activity was disrupted by the alert level changes this quarter, with New Zealand in alert level 4 for 14 days and mixed alert levels across the country for 30 days.
Alert level changes in the quarter:
- 18 August 2021: New Zealand moves to alert level 4.
- 1 September 2021: All of New Zealand south of Auckland moves to alert level 3. Auckland and Northland remain at alert level 4.
- 3 September 2021: Northland moves to alert level 3. New Zealand (except Auckland) is now at alert level 3. Auckland remains at alert level 4.
- 8 September 2021: New Zealand (except Auckland) moves to alert level 2.
- 22 September 2021: Auckland and Upper Hauraki move to alert level 3. Rest of New Zealand remains at alert level 2.
- 26 September 2021: Upper Hauraki moves to alert level 2. Auckland remains at alert level 3. The rest of New Zealand remains at alert level 2.
History of the COVID-19 Alert System has more information on restrictions and COVID-19 alert changes.
During the September 2021 quarter the total days at various alert levels were:
- New Zealand was in alert level 1 for 48 days, or approximately 52 percent
- New Zealand was in alert level 4 for 14 days, or approximately 15 percent
- New Zealand was in mixed alert level 3 and 4 for 7 days, or approximately 8 percent
- New Zealand was in mixed alert level 2 and 4 for 14 days, or approximately 15 percent
- New Zealand was in mixed alert level 2 and 3 for 9 days, or approximately 10 percent.

### Data and methodology considerations
The abrupt economic shock caused by COVID-19 and measures to contain it posed challenges to our normal methods of measuring economic activity. The measurement of some GDP(P) and GDP(E) components will be impacted by the COVID-19 restrictions over the September 2021 quarter, after having not been impacted by any large-scale alert level changes over the last few quarters. We will review all components in the September 2021 quarter and where appropriate, will use alternative data and methodologies in cases where conditions support it.
We released information on measurement challenges and our approach to these in the following papers:
June 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
March 2021 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
December 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
September 2020 quarter gross domestic product and COVID-19
June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19: Updated
June 2020 quarter Gross domestic product and COVID-19
COVID-19 lockdown and gross domestic product for March and June 2020 quarters
We used alternative data sources in place of some of our usual data sources and methodologies in the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and the March, June, and September 2021 quarters to more accurately capture quarterly changes in activity. This was applied in the following cases:
- for components with methods that were unable to capture the sharp changes in activity that occurred due to the alert level 3 and 4 lockdowns (for example employment-based indicators, which were affected by the wage subsidy scheme)
- for components that experienced sharp changes in relationships between output and intermediate consumption
- for components that have a method of interpolation without a quarterly indicator.
During the September 2020, December 2020, and the March 2021 quarters we reviewed and reverted some components back to their usual quarterly method. These will be reviewed in the September 2021 quarter and where necessary, will again use alternative indicators to capture the impacts of the higher restriction levels.
The use of alternative indicators is summarised in the Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments . We will provide a further update on the approaches being used for GDP(P) and GDP(E) components on 10 December 2021, ahead of the release of Gross domestic product: September 2021 quarter .

### Revisions to GDP(P) and GDP(E) components
As more data becomes available, we continue to review our approach to measuring GDP during the COVID-19-affected quarters. As part of this ongoing review process, we have identified some areas where we can improve our measurement over the COVID-19-affected period.
In the September 2021 quarter release of the gross domestic product, we will also be incorporating the latest balanced annual national accounts and implementing updates to quarterly methodologies. This process occurs every September quarter and results in revisions to previously published series.
Improvements to gross domestic product: September 2021 quarter has more details on incorporating the latest balanced annual national accounts data.

#### Implementing updates to quarterly methodologies
We have updated quarterly methodology for a number of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components. These are outlined in the Improvements to gross domestic product: September 2021 quarter paper.
These updates impact the following components:
- owner-occupied property operation and rental and hiring services (except real estate) in the rental, hiring, and real estate services industry
- household consumption expenditure (HCE)
- gross fixed capital formation (GFKF).

#### Change to COVID-19 alternative indicator used for residential-care services and social assistance
We have implemented a change to the alternative indicator used to capture the impacts of COVID-19 on residential-care services and social assistance. We are now using a composite indicator derived from low-level household labour force survey data and monthly employment indicator data as the alternative indicator. This will be used for the March 2020 and June 2020 quarters. We will revert to the usual quarterly indicator (salaries and wages data deflated by a labour cost index) from the September 2020 quarter and onwards. This change will also result in revisions to household consumption expenditure on welfare services.

#### Change to COVID-19 alternative indicator used for heritage and artistic activities
We have implemented a change to the alternative indicator used to capture the impacts of COVID-19 on heritage and artistic activities. We are now using a composite indicator derived from low-level household labour force survey data and monthly employment indicator data as the alternative indicator. This will be used for the March 2020 to September 2021 quarters. This change will also result in revisions to household consumption expenditure on cultural services.

#### Changes to components using alternative data sources due to COVID-19
As part of our ongoing review process, and influenced by changes in source data and the incorporation of annual data, we have changed the use of alternative data sources in some areas. These changes are to the following components:
- construction services – moved to using the BFD value- added alternative indicator in the March and June 2021 quarters, where we had previously reverted to the usual method.
- other private health out-patient services component of household consumption expenditure - reverted to usual method in the quarters from September 2020 through to June 2021.

#### Changes in seasonal adjustment of imports and exports
Historically, the current price and constant price measures of imports of goods and services, and exports of goods and services have used different approaches to seasonal adjustment. The constant price series has used a direct method. The current price series used an indirect method, which is consistent with the method used in the related balance of payments current price series. This has worked well because the two methods have produced similar outputs while preserving additivity in the current price series. However, when components see unusually high or low levels of activity, as is currently the case, the output from direct and indirect methods are very different. This has resulted in undesirable and unanticipated impacts on our implicit price deflators (IPDS).
To address these issues, we will now use direct seasonal adjustment for both current and constant prices series. This change will lead to revisions to the following series:
- imports of goods and services, seasonally adjusted current price series
- exports of goods and services, seasonally adjusted current price series
- imports of goods and services, implicit price deflators seasonally adjusted series
- exports of goods and services, implicit price deflators seasonally adjusted series.
This change allows us to maintain coherence between current and constant price series and will produce sensible implicit price deflators. However, note that additivity of the current price series will no longer hold. Additionally, changing the current price series to direct method, rather than changing the constant price series to an indirect method, better captures the full impact of closed borders upon seasonally adjusted imports and exports.
Note that these changes do not have an impact on aggregate GDP(E) as it is directly seasonally adjusted.
Quarterly gross domestic product: Sources and methods (fifth edition) has more information about direct and indirect seasonal adjustment.

#### Challenges with measuring low-level breakdowns of household consumption expenditure
Our methodology for compiling low-level breakdowns of quarterly household consumption expenditure (HCE) is to measure total domestic spending for that category, then add New Zealand tourist expenditure overseas (travel debits) and remove overseas tourist expenditure in New Zealand (travel credits) to get HCE.
We use fixed proportions to split travel credits and debits into HCE categories. These proportions change based on commodity information from the annual supply-use balancing process but are generally quite stable over time and mostly based on historical information. As a result, these proportions are an area of weakness in our measurement. Since the June 2020 quarter we have made some use of electronic card transactions data to modify the proportions used to split travel credits into HCE categories. This allows us to account for some changes in spending patterns during the COVID-19-affected period. This approach was not possible for travel debits, due to a lack of data.
The abrupt changes in activity as a result of COVID-19 impacts have highlighted some limitations in our historical apportionment of travel credits to HCE classes. In recent quarters some HCE categories, notably hotels and accommodation, and recreation and cultural services, have seen quite large seasonally adjusted increases. These categories have previously had a relatively large portion of travel credits allocated to them in pre-COVID-19 times. This suggests that our pre-COVID-19 travel credit portions are likely to have been overestimated in these categories, and that consequently our HCE spending in these categories was previously underestimated. This mismeasurement affects the seasonal factors applied to these categories.
The large COVID-19-induced shifts in spending in categories such as hotels and accommodation, and recreation and cultural services (away from tourists, and toward spending by New Zealand residents) have hence translated into some strong seasonally adjusted movements for these HCE categories. While there have been strong increases, the extent of the increases is likely overstated in seasonally adjusted terms. We therefore advise caution when using data at this level of disaggregation.
Note that total HCE and total GDP(E) are not affected by these issues.

#### Employment indicators
Paid-hours data sourced from the quarterly employment survey (QES) are used as a quarterly indicator for a range of GDP(P) and GDP(E) components. The use of this data source to represent activity has two main limitations in the September 2021 quarter:
- It is a mid-quarter estimate, and so for September 2021 quarter will represent pay periods ending on or immediately before 20 August 2021. The June 2021 quarter QES represented pay periods ending on or immediately before 20 May 2021.
- QES paid-hours data may deviate substantially from hours worked due to the COVID-19 wage subsidy, and so may not be a suitable proxy for activity in the September 2021 quarter.
Quarterly employment survey – DataInfo+ has information about the methodology used in this survey.
Gross-earnings data sourced from the employer monthly schedule (EMS) is also used in some components. This data source will also include the wage subsidy and may not be a suitable proxy for activity in the September 2021 quarter.
We will continue to monitor our use of employment indicators such as hours paid from the QES and gross earnings from the employer monthly schedule (EMS) and confront these against other data sources such as the business financial data (BFD), wage subsidy data, and the composite household labour force survey/employment indicator that we used in the June 2020 quarter. Of note, the QES results for the September 2021 quarter represent pay periods up to and including 20 August 2021, and so may not fully reflect the impact of higher alert levels in the latter half of the September 2021 quarter. Where appropriate we will replace the QES indicator with the same alternative data source used in the June 2020 quarter or previous quarters.
We will continue our use of additive outliers to treat unusual data points and will be testing the September 2021 quarter series to determine where additive outliers need to be applied. Previous outliers applied to the March, June, September, and December 2020 quarters and/or the March and June 2021 quarters results will be retained in the upcoming release of Gross domestic product: September 2021 quarter .

#### Seasonal adjustment of travel services and the September 2021 quarter
New Zealand’s border has been closed to almost all travellers since 19 March 2020, with the exception of New Zealand citizens, residents with valid travel conditions, and those granted approval to travel.
Exports of travel services make a significant contribution to New Zealand’s economy. Prior to COVID-19 the peak travel season fell in summer. In the 2019 calendar year, March quarter exports of travel services were worth $5.5 billion, compared with around $4.0 billion in the December quarter, $3.5 billion in the June quarter, and $3.0 billion in the September quarter.
The restrictions on travel to New Zealand in place since March 2020 mean that the value of travel service exports have declined significantly to between 1.0 and 1.5 billion in June 2021 and in all subsequent quarters.
As we have highlighted before in previous quarters, the change from normal seasonal patterns is effectively captured in seasonally adjusted aggregate GDP(E), meaning the full seasonal impact of reduced international tourism will be reflected in total GDP(E). However, the same impact will not be reflected in lower-level components, such as exports of travel services, because the multiplicative decomposition method used in seasonal adjustment means that smaller seasonal adjustments are made to components that have fallen in size.
Because both June and September quarters typically have low levels of travel activity, we expect the impact of reduced international tourism on September 2021 seasonally adjusted growth to be smaller than what was observed in the March 2021 and June 2021 quarters
In GDP(P), travel-related activity is distributed across a range of industries and appears to have a softer seasonal contribution than what we observe in GDP(E).

### Corrections to quarterly gross domestic product

#### Correction to the food and beverage services subindustry
We have identified and corrected an error in the application of the alternative quarterly indicator used for the food and beverage services subindustry over the COVID-19 affected period (March 2020 quarter to the June 2021 quarter).
We missed applying the additive outlier treatment to the seasonally adjusted data used as the alternative indicator for this industry. This correction has resulted in revisions to the quarterly growth rates for this industry and higher-level aggregates from the March 2020 quarter to the June 2021 quarter. The revisions due to this correction range from negative $80 million to positive $60 million in the retail trade and accommodation industry. The most significant revisions to quarterly growth will be in the June 2021 quarter, with a downward revision of $80 million (seasonally adjusted, constant price terms) to both the accommodation and food services subindustry, and the retail trade and accommodation industry. Please note that these approximate impacts are indicative only, and there may be other revisions that are introduced during the compilation of Gross domestic product: September 2021 quarter .
Tessa Magson 04 931 4600 info@stats.govt.nz
ISBN 978-1-99-003281-3
- Gross domestic product
- Overview of sources and methods for quarterly gross domestic product: Updates and COVID-19 adjustments 19 March 2026
